The article mentions that Houston is on the list, and the reasons given include factors like the number of fast food restaurants, high humidity, poor air quality, and long commutes. The editor-in-chief, Neal Boulton, points out that fast food restaurants being twice the national average contribute. Also, the magazine's methodology included looking at factors such as the number and types of restaurants, park space, air quality, weather, and health clubs. So, the key here is to see which of these factors align with the answer choices. Option B says the number of fast food restaurants is a factor, which is definitely part of it. These all seem to be factors related to unhealthy lifestyles.
